text: The laparoscopic view reveals fibrinous exudates and fluid collection throughout the abdominal quadrants, indicative of a ruptured appendix. The surgeon performs suction irrigation and thorough lavage of the peritoneal cavity. The omentum, which is sealing the appendix, is carefully separated from the surrounding adhesions.

In the pelvic cavity, the surgeon uses suction to remove excess fluid. The bladder is visible, and the appendix is identified. A Stryker mini alligator is used to gently lift the appendix, which is ruptured at the base, while separating the surrounding omentum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to identify the perforation at the base of the appendix, which is visible on the left side. The cecum is located on the right side of the patient. The perforation is evident, with the appendix stump attached to the cecum. A Mishra's knot is applied using a striker mini alligator to gently secure the appendix without causing further amputation. The knot is tightened with minimal force to ensure it encompasses the perforated area.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to amputate the appendix, applying heat to cauterize the mucosa and prevent mucosal leakage. The infected appendix is then removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon performs thorough irrigation and suction in the abdominal cavity, using approximately three liters of saline for lavage to ensure proper cleaning and minimize peritonitis. An air bubble test is conducted to check for leaks. A drain is placed, and the appendix is retrieved using an endo bag.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation in all quadrants of the abdomen, with the patient's head positioned downward to facilitate fluid drainage. The striker mini alligator holds the appendix, which will be removed in an endo bag. The applied knot is visible near the cecum, with the tinea coli and gallbladder also identifiable in the surgical field. No leakage is observed in the area, ensuring thorough lavage to prevent subdiaphragmatic abscess formation.

The surgeon performs repeated suction irrigation throughout the abdominal quadrants, rubbing away fibrinous exudate to maximize washout and aspiration. Visible fluid is aspirated from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon completes peritoneal toileting and introduces a glove as an endo bag into the abdominal cavity. The appendix is placed into the endo bag, and the edges of the glove are held with an instrument to pull it through the cannula. The entire cannula is removed, and a nasogastric tube is introduced as a drain. The port is then closed following tissue extraction through a ten-millimeter extra port.
